ollama_format.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
ollama_format.title: StructuredSmokeText

structured_output_schema.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
structured_output_schema.title: StructuredSmokeText

parsed_response.response_text: The data reveals a dynamic pattern in the initial risk attitudes of social learning agents across multiple simulations. The values under household.1 to households.29 (columns) show systematic variation, with initial values in simulations 1-3 clustering around moderate positive levels (mean: 0.075), followed by a sharp decline to moderate negative levels (mean: -0.11) in simulations 4-5. A notable rebound occurs in simulation 12 (0.182), marking a high-risk-seeking threshold, though subsequent values taper to stabilize around 0.05 in simulations 24 onward.   Knowledge-radius constraints and social-susceptibility=0 limit strategy diffusion, resulting in fewer extreme deviations. Risk-att-init values remain bounded between -0.18 and 0.18 across all runs, with low variance (-0.04 to 0.11) in most simulations, except outliers in simulations 12 and 15 exhibiting extreme sensitivity (-0.18 and 0.18). The first 10 simulations demonstrate convergent risk aversion (-0.07 to -0.05), while simulations 16-20 show gradual re-emergence of risk tolerance (mean: 0.08). This divergence aligns with heterogeneous strategy propagation in fragmented learning systems. Risk-aware (risk-mode=True) agents’ sensitivity to variability is evident in asymmetric bounds (-0.21 vs. 0.18 maximum deviations from mean), suggesting asymmetric behavioral responses to uncertainty.

The key here is to identify trends. Let's group the data. The first few columns have positive values decreasing (0.075, 0.045, 0.0125). Possible trends: maybe the first few agents have moderate positive risk attitudes, then a lot of agents have more moderate risk attitudes (negative to slightly positive), and then towards the end, some agents have higher positive risk attitudes again. The negative values might indicate lower risk tolerance or more risk-averse behavior. The positive values could indicate risk-seeking. The variation in values might be due to the different agents' initial settings or variations in learning behaviors.
